ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to lavash exists; 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315873) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a lavash-specific code; A000E (bread and similar products) is the closest defensible anchor within the grain/bread hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code specific to lavash bread identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser as of 2026-04.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large piece of lavash bread weighing approximately 150 g, typically used as a wrap for kebab or shawarma or served alongside dips.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bread, lavash (SR Legacy ID 18033 / Foundation Foods); cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy flatbread entries
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) — Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for commercially prepared white-flour lavash bread used as the primary nutrient anchor. This is an approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values are broadly consistent with the input record (GrabFood-derived, confidence 0.7). Sodium at 400 mg/100 g is within the range reported for commercially prepared lavash (350–450 mg/100 g) in USDA SR Legacy. Potassium estimated at 110 mg/100 g from USDA SR Legacy data for similar white-flour flatbreads. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not individually reported in the primary source and set to -1. The GrabFood-derived input source is not used as a primary nutrient evidence source; USDA FoodData Central is the sole approved nutrient authority for this record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. University of Sydney GI Database — white-flour flatbread / pita analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Diabetes Association — Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No lavash-specific GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 71 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white-flour flatbread and pita bread (GI range 68–75, mean ~71) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed international GI table (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care, 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Both sources are approved per Project Veritas v2 GI source policy. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving / 100) = 71 × (50.0 g/100 g × 150 g serving / 100) = 71 × 0.75 = 53.25.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium (110 mg/100 g) and fibre (2.0 g/100 g) offset
hypertension.guidance: Lavash bread contains 400 mg sodium per 100 g — at the upper borderline threshold — with potassium below the 200 mg/100 g offset requirement and fibre below 3 g/100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, choose low-sodium preparations, and pair with potassium-rich accompaniments such as avocado or hummus.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~71) and 50 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 2.0 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: White-flour lavash has a high GI (~71) and delivers approximately 75 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate per standard 150 g serving, causing significant postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size, substitute wholemeal lavash to increase fibre above 3 g/100 g, and pair with protein-rich or high-fibre toppings to blunt glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (400 mg/100 g), low saturated fat (0.67 g/100 g), and refined carbohydrate with low fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lavash bread is low in saturated fat (0.67 g/100 g) and contains no trans fat, which is favorable for cardiovascular health, but its high sodium content (400 mg/100 g) and refined carbohydrate profile with low fibre (2.0 g/100 g) contribute to cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit sodium intake, choose wholemeal variants, and keep serving sizes moderate.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with low fibre and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: White-flour lavash provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3s, polyphenols, or fermented cultures) and its refined carbohydrate content may contribute to glycemic load-driven inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer wholemeal lavash and pair it with anti-inflammatory toppings such as extra-virgin olive oil, turmeric hummus, or oily fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lavash bread is a traditional soft, thin, and unleavened flatbread originating from Turkey, Armenia, and the broader Middle East, where it has been a dietary staple for centuries. It is made from a simple dough of white wheat flour, water, salt, and a small amount of oil, then baked at high heat — traditionally on a convex griddle (saj) or in a tandoor oven — producing a pliable, slightly chewy sheet with a mild, neutral flavour and a lightly blistered surface. In Singapore and Southeast Asia, lavash is commonly served as an accompaniment to Turkish kebab and shawarma dishes, used as a wrap for grilled meats and vegetables, or offered alongside dips such as hummus and tzatziki. Nutritionally, white-flour lavash provides approximately 266 kcal, 8 g protein, 4 g fat, and 50 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a relatively low saturated fat content (0.67 g/100 g) and no trans fat. However, its dietary fibre content is low at 2.0 g/100 g and its sodium content is elevated at 400 mg/100 g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. Its high glycemic index (~71) means it causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation, particularly relevant for diabetic individuals. Wholemeal lavash variants offer improved fibre and a lower glycemic response. Lavash is naturally vegan, vegetarian, and halal, and contains gluten as a primary allergen.

Is lavash bread suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for commercially prepared white-flour lavash bread, an approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. The input record was AI-generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood data, which is a forbidden source and is not used as primary nutrient evidence. GI is an analogous estimate from white-flour flatbread/pita data in the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI table. Potassium is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for similar flatbreads. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown, and several micronutrients, are not individually reported and are set to -1.
notes: 1. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for commercially prepared lavash bread is not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04. 2. Water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific lavash preparation is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy source consulted. 3. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not individually reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy source for this food item as of 2026-04. 4. Trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for commercially prepared white-flour lavash. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as lavash contains no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for white-flour bread products. 5. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white-flour flatbread/pita bread data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care; no lavash-specific GI entry exists in the database. Glycemic load per serving (150 g) = 71 × (75 g carbohydrate / 100) = 53.25. 6. Potassium estimated at 110 mg/100 g from USDA SR Legacy data for similar white-flour flatbreads; this value should be confirmed against a direct lavash analysis if available. 7. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, reflecting the GrabFood listing context; the true culinary origin of lavash is Turkey/Armenia/Middle East, which is reflected in cuisine and region_of_origin fields. 8. Multilingual local names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as lavash is sold in Singapore as part of Turkish/kebab cuisine offerings. 9. GrabFood is a forbidden source per Project Veritas v2 source policy and has not been used as primary nutrient evidence; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the sole approved nutrient authority for this record.
